steps.title: Identify the punctuation boundary after the parenthetical
steps.content: The blank comes immediately after the parenthetical “(i.e., fungi that live inside a host plant)” and before “researching 120 fungi–produced compounds, she found...”. The challenge is punctuating this transition correctly.

steps.title: Apply the sentence boundary rule
steps.content: A period after the closing parenthesis correctly marks the end of the first sentence and begins a new sentence with an introductory prepositional phrase (“By researching”) separated from the main clause by a comma. The other options either use improper punctuation (comma without a period fails to mark the boundary; no punctuation leaves it ambiguous), introduce an unparalleled conjunction (and), or create an awkward, ungrammatical structure. That matches choice A — A is correct.
